title: Hyponatremia

summary: Hyponatremia is defined as serum sodium <135 mEq/L and is the most common electrolyte disorder encountered in clinical practice, affecting up to 15–30% of hospitalised patients.
The most immediately life-threatening complication is cerebral oedema from rapid water influx into brain cells, causing herniation; this is most dangerous in acute hyponatremia (<48 hours).
Classification by volume status (hypovolaemic, euvolaemic, hypervolaemic) is the essential first step in management and guides fluid therapy.
SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion) is the most common cause of euvolaemic hyponatremia; causes include CNS disease, pulmonary disease, drugs, and malignancy.
In India, common causes include diarrhoeal dehydration with hypotonic fluid replacement, diuretic use, tuberculosis-related SIADH, and hypothyroidism.
Correction rate is critical: chronic hyponatremia must be corrected slowly (≤8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS/CPM).
Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl) is reserved for symptomatic acute hyponatremia (seizures, altered consciousness) — use with extreme caution and close monitoring.
Correction of the underlying cause is always the primary goal; sodium correction alone without treating the aetiology will result in recurrence.

qna.question: What is the safe correction rate for chronic hyponatremia?
qna.answer: No more than 8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours (some guidelines recommend ≤10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours). Exceeding this risks osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS), an irreversible pontine injury. In high-risk patients (malnourished, alcoholics, hypokalemia), limit to ≤8 mEq/L/24h.

qna.question: How do you classify hyponatremia by volume status?
qna.answer: Hypovolaemic (volume-depleted: dry mucous membranes, skin tenting, tachycardia — causes: diarrhoea, vomiting, diuretics, Addison's). Euvolaemic (no oedema, normal skin turgor — causes: SIADH, hypothyroidism, glucocorticoid deficiency, polydipsia). Hypervolaemic (oedema, ascites — causes: heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure).

qna.question: What is SIADH and how is it diagnosed?
qna.answer: SIADH = inappropriate ADH secretion → renal water retention → dilutional hyponatremia. Diagnostic criteria: serum Na <135, serum osmolality <275, urine Na >20 mEq/L, urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg, clinically euvolaemic, no diuretics, normal thyroid/adrenal function.

qna.question: When is 3% hypertonic saline indicated?
qna.answer: Only in symptomatic acute hyponatremia: seizures, severe altered consciousness, respiratory failure from cerebral oedema. Give 100–150 mL of 3% NaCl over 15–20 minutes, may repeat 1–2 times. TARGET: raise Na by 3–5 mEq/L to control symptoms. Never use in chronic asymptomatic hyponatremia.

qna.question: What is pseudohyponatremia and how do you identify it?
qna.answer: Pseudohyponatremia occurs when hyperproteinaemia (myeloma) or hyperlipidaemia artificially lower measured Na by older flame photometry methods. Plasma osmolality is NORMAL (275–295 mOsm/kg). Ion-selective electrode measurement shows true normal sodium. No treatment needed.

qna.question: What are the symptoms of hyponatremia and when are they life-threatening?
qna.answer: Mild (Na 130–134): often asymptomatic, fatigue, nausea. Moderate (Na 125–129): headache, lethargy, cognitive slowing. Severe (<125 or rapid drop): confusion, seizures, coma, respiratory arrest. Severity correlates with rate of drop more than absolute value — acute fall of 10 mEq/L can cause cerebral herniation.

qna.question: How do you manage hyponatremia in heart failure?
qna.answer: Treat underlying heart failure (ACE inhibitors, diuretics, fluid restriction <1–1.5 L/day). Free water restriction 500–1000 mL/day. Stop diuretics temporarily if severe hyponatremia. Tolvaptan (vasopressin antagonist) can be used but is expensive and requires specialist supervision. Target: improve cardiac output, not aggressive sodium correction.

qna.question: Which drugs commonly cause hyponatremia?
qna.answer: SSRIs (most common drug cause in elderly), thiazide diuretics (NOT loop diuretics), carbamazepine, valproate, cyclophosphamide, vincristine, MDMA/ecstasy, oxytocin, desmopressin (DDAVP), NSAIDs (potentiate ADH). Always take a full drug history in every patient with hyponatremia.

definition.text: Hyponatremia is defined as a serum sodium concentration below 135 mEq/L (135 mmol/L). It represents a relative excess of water compared to sodium in the extracellular fluid and is the most common electrolyte abnormality in hospitalised patients. It may be acute (<48 hours) or chronic (≥48 hours or unknown duration), and the distinction critically affects management.
definition.keyThreshold: Na <135 mEq/L: hyponatremia. Na <125 mEq/L: severe, high risk of symptoms. Na <120 mEq/L: critical, risk of cerebral herniation

categories.category: Hypovolaemic Hyponatremia (low total body Na, lower total body water)
causes: Gastrointestinal losses: diarrhoea, vomiting, fistula, NG suction
Renal losses: thiazide diuretics, Addison's disease (primary adrenal insufficiency), cerebral salt wasting, osmotic diuresis
Third-space losses: pancreatitis, burns, trauma, peritonitis
Skin losses: excessive sweating with hypotonic replacement

categories.category: Euvolaemic Hyponatremia (normal total body Na, excess water)
causes: SIADH (syndrome of inappropriate ADH): CNS disease (meningitis, SAH, stroke, TB meningitis), pulmonary disease (pneumonia, TB, empyema), malignancy (small cell lung cancer), drugs
Beer potomania (low solute intake + high water intake)
SIADH-inducing drugs: SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, oxytocin

categories.category: Hypervolaemic Hyponatremia (high total body Na, higher total body water excess)
causes: Congestive heart failure (reduced effective arterial volume → baroreceptor-mediated ADH release)
Advanced chronic kidney disease / renal failure
Hyperglycaemia (glucose draws water into extracellular space → dilutional; correct by adding 1.6 mEq/L Na for each 100 mg/dL glucose >100)
Pseudohyponatremia: severe hyperproteinaemia (myeloma), severe hyperlipidaemia — laboratory artefact only

riskFactors: Age >65 years (impaired renal concentrating ability, polypharmacy)
Female sex (higher risk of ODS from correction)
SSRI/antidepressant use (common and underrecognised cause in elderly)
Hospitalisation (IV hypotonic fluids, pain, nausea — all stimulate ADH)
Tuberculosis (TB meningitis, pulmonary TB — common India-specific risk)
commonCauses: Hypovolaemic: diarrhoea with hypotonic fluid replacement (most common in India — rural settings)
Euvolaemic: SIADH (drugs — SSRIs, carbamazepine — or TB meningitis/pulmonary TB)
rareCauses: Cerebral salt wasting (after SAH or brain injury)

pathophysiology.summary: Hyponatremia is fundamentally a disorder of water homeostasis, not sodium homeostasis. The body regulates serum osmolality between 275–295 mOsm/kg via ADH (vasopressin) secretion from the posterior pituitary and renal water handling. When serum osmolality falls, ADH is normally suppressed → free water excretion → osmolality normalises. Hyponatremia develops when this mechanism fails: either ADH is inappropriately elevated (SIADH, heart failure, cirrhosis), or water intake massively exceeds excretory capacity (polydipsia). As serum Na falls, water enters cells down an osmotic gradient → cellular swelling. In the brain, this causes cerebral oedema. In chronic hyponatremia (>48 hours), brain cells compensate by extruding organic osmoles (taurine, myoinositol, glutamate) — this adaptation reduces cerebral oedema but means rapid correction will dehydrate these now-adjusted cells, causing osmotic demyelination of pontine fibres (central pontine myelinolysis/ODS).
keyMechanisms: Inappropriate ADH secretion or action → renal free water retention → dilutional hyponatremia
Reduced effective arterial volume (heart failure, cirrhosis) → baroreceptor activation → ADH release → water retention despite low Na
Water shift from intracellular to extracellular: hyperglycaemia raises extracellular osmolality → water moves out → dilutes Na
Brain cell swelling in acute hyponatremia → cerebral oedema → herniation (can occur within hours)
Brain cell adaptation in chronic hyponatremia (organic osmole extrusion) → rapid correction → osmotic demyelination
Thiazides block NaCl reabsorption in distal tubule but preserve ADH-mediated water reabsorption → Na depleted, water retained
pathophysiology.clinicalRelevance: The rate of development matters more than the absolute level. Acute hyponatremia (developing in <48h) has no time for brain adaptation — even moderate hyponatremia can cause life-threatening cerebral oedema. Chronic hyponatremia (>48h) is better tolerated but carries the reverse risk during treatment: correct too fast → osmotic demyelination → quadriplegia, locked-in syndrome, death. Always determine chronicity before starting treatment.

symptoms.feature: Gait instability, increased fall risk (independent of symptoms)

signs.feature: Dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, tachycardia, hypotension
Pitting oedema, raised JVP, ascites, basal crackles
signs.note: Hypervolaemic hyponatremia — heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic

signs.feature: No oedema, normal skin turgor, no orthostatic hypotension
Reduced deep tendon reflexes, bradycardia, periorbital oedema, slow relaxing reflexes, dry skin, coarse voice
Neurological signs — papilloedema, focal deficits, meningism
signs.note: CNS cause of SIADH (TB meningitis, SAH)

signs.feature: Dysarthria, dysphagia, flaccid quadriparesis, pseudobulbar palsy — after rapid correction

redFlags: Seizures or coma in context of low sodium → EMERGENCY: 3% hypertonic saline immediately, ICU transfer
Sodium <120 mEq/L regardless of symptoms → treat as emergency even if apparently asymptomatic
Postural hypotension + hyponatremia + hyperpigmentation → rule out Addison's disease urgently (give hydrocortisone)
Hyponatremia + meningism + fever → TB meningitis until proven otherwise (lumbar puncture, start ATT)
New neurological deficit after sodium correction began → osmotic demyelination — STOP correction, discuss with nephrology
Hyponatremia + weight loss + haemoptysis + smoking history → SIADH from small cell lung cancer

severity.criteria: Na 130–134 mEq/L, asymptomatic or mild symptoms (fatigue, nausea), chronic
severity.management: Identify and treat underlying cause. Fluid restrict if SIADH. Avoid rapid correction. Outpatient if stable.

severity.criteria: Na 125–129 mEq/L, or symptomatic (headache, confusion, vomiting), chronic
severity.management: Admit for monitoring. Treat cause. Isotonic saline if hypovolaemic. Fluid restriction if euvolaemic/hypervolaemic. Correct ≤8–10 mEq/L/24h.

severity.criteria: Na <125 mEq/L with severe symptoms (seizures, coma, respiratory distress) regardless of chronicity
severity.management: ICU admission. 3% NaCl 100–150 mL bolus IV over 15–20 min. Repeat until symptoms resolve (target +3–5 mEq/L). Then slow correction. Do NOT exceed 10 mEq/L in 24h.

stepByStep.action: Confirm hyponatremia and exclude pseudohyponatremia: check serum osmolality. If normal (275–295), consider hyperproteinaemia or hyperlipidaemia as artefact.
stepByStep.rationale: True hyponatremia = low serum osmolality (<275 mOsm/kg). Normal osmolality with low Na = pseudohyponatremia — no treatment needed.

stepByStep.action: Check serum glucose: if hyperglycaemic, correct measured Na (add 1.6 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose above 100). This is hypertonic hyponatremia — treat hyperglycaemia, not sodium.
stepByStep.rationale: Hyperglycaemia causes water shift out of cells → dilutes sodium. Correcting glucose restores sodium.

stepByStep.action: Assess clinical volume status: examine for postural hypotension, skin turgor, mucous membranes, JVP, oedema, ascites, pulmonary crackles.
stepByStep.rationale: Volume status is the single most important step — determines whether to give fluid (hypovolaemic), restrict fluid (euvolaemic/hypervolaemic), or treat heart failure/cirrhosis.

stepByStep.action: Send urine sodium and urine osmolality. Urine Na >20 mEq/L with euvolaemia → SIADH. Urine Na <20 mEq/L → hypovolaemia or poor cardiac output.
stepByStep.rationale: Urine Na distinguishes renal from extra-renal causes and confirms SIADH.

stepByStep.action: If euvolaemic with urine Na >20 and urine osmolality >100: work up for SIADH — check TSH (hypothyroidism), 8AM cortisol (adrenal insufficiency), chest X-ray (TB, malignancy), full drug history.
stepByStep.rationale: SIADH is a diagnosis of exclusion — must rule out hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency first as both mimic SIADH.

stepByStep.action: Determine chronicity: acute (<48 hours) vs chronic (≥48 hours or unknown). Hospitalised patients with new hyponatremia after a procedure or drug → likely acute. Chronic symptoms, stable community patient → likely chronic.
stepByStep.rationale: Chronicity determines safe correction rate. Acute = can correct faster (up to 1–2 mEq/L/hour initially for life-threatening symptoms). Chronic = must be slow (max 8–10 mEq/L/24h).

stepByStep.action: Investigate underlying cause of SIADH if confirmed: CT chest/abdomen (malignancy), CSF if meningism (TB meningitis), ANA/ANCA if vasculitis suspected. Review all medications.
stepByStep.rationale: Treating the underlying cause is the definitive treatment for SIADH.

keyInvestigations.name: Serum electrolytes (Na, K, Cl, HCO3)
keyInvestigations.purpose: Confirm hyponatremia, detect associated hypokalaemia (thiazides, vomiting) or hyperkalemia (Addison's)
keyInvestigations.interpretation: Na <135: hyponatremia. K <3.5 with diuretics: thiazide-induced. K >5.5 with low Na: consider Addison's.

keyInvestigations.name: Serum osmolality
keyInvestigations.purpose: Confirm true vs pseudohyponatremia; classify as hypotonic, isotonic, or hypertonic
keyInvestigations.interpretation: <275 mOsm/kg: true hypotonic hyponatremia. 275–295: pseudohyponatremia or hypertonic cause. >295: hyperglycaemia or mannitol.

keyInvestigations.name: Urine sodium (spot)
keyInvestigations.purpose: Distinguish renal from extra-renal water/Na loss; confirm SIADH
keyInvestigations.interpretation: Urine Na <20 mEq/L: extra-renal Na loss (diarrhoea, vomiting, burns) or poor cardiac output. Urine Na >20 mEq/L with euvolaemia: SIADH. Urine Na >20 with hypovolaemia: renal loss (diuretics, Addison's).

keyInvestigations.name: Urine osmolality
keyInvestigations.purpose: Assess concentrating ability; essential for SIADH diagnosis
keyInvestigations.interpretation: <100 mOsm/kg: primary polydipsia (appropriately dilute urine). >100 mOsm/kg with hyponatremia: ADH effect present (SIADH if euvolaemic).

keyInvestigations.name: Serum glucose
keyInvestigations.interpretation: Correct Na by adding 1.6 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose above 100 mg/dL. If corrected Na is normal, treat hyperglycaemia.

keyInvestigations.name: TSH
keyInvestigations.purpose: Exclude hypothyroidism as cause of SIADH-like picture
keyInvestigations.interpretation: TSH >4.5 mIU/L: hypothyroidism. Treat thyroid → sodium will normalise.

keyInvestigations.name: 8AM serum cortisol
keyInvestigations.purpose: Exclude adrenal insufficiency (Addison's or secondary) as cause of hyponatremia
keyInvestigations.interpretation: <140 nmol/L: likely adrenal insufficiency. >500 nmol/L: excludes. 140–500: needs ACTH stimulation test.

keyInvestigations.name: Chest X-ray
keyInvestigations.purpose: Screen for pulmonary TB, small cell lung cancer (causes SIADH), pneumonia
keyInvestigations.interpretation: Hilar adenopathy + upper lobe infiltrate: TB. Hilar mass: small cell lung cancer.

diagnosticApproach.diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Serum osmolality normal? → pseudohyponatremia or hypertonic (hyperglycaemia). Step 2: Serum osmolality low? → TRUE hyponatremia → assess volume status. Step 3: HYPOVOLAEMIC (dry, tachycardic)? → urine Na <20: extra-renal loss (GI, skin). Urine Na >20: renal/adrenal loss (diuretics, Addison's). Step 4: EUVOLAEMIC? → TSH, cortisol → if normal: SIADH → urine Na >20, urine Osm >100. Step 5: HYPERVOLAEMIC (oedematous)? → CHF / cirrhosis / nephrotic / renal failure.

differentialDiagnosis.distinguishingFeature: Euvolaemic, urine Na >20, urine osmolality >100, normal TSH/cortisol, identifiable cause (drug, CNS, pulmonary)
Euvolaemic, elevated TSH, clinical features (bradycardia, periorbital oedema, cold intolerance, slow reflexes)
Hypovolaemic, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia, low cortisol, hypotension
Hypervolaemic, raised JVP, bilateral oedema, basal crackles, reduced LVEF on echo
Hypervolaemic, ascites, spider naevi, palmar erythema, raised LFTs, low albumin
Euvolaemic, urine osmolality <100 mOsm/kg (maximally dilute urine), history of >8–10 L/day water intake, psychiatric history
Hypovolaemic or euvolaemic, hypokalemia, clear drug history, urine Na elevated (renal sodium wasting)
SIADH pattern, meningism, fever, lymphocytic pleocytosis on CSF, elevated ADA in CSF, India context

principles: The RATE of correction is more important than the absolute sodium level — never exceed 10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours in chronic hyponatremia.
Treat the underlying cause — sodium will correct spontaneously once the aetiology is addressed (e.g., stop thiazide, treat TB, start levothyroxine).
Match treatment to volume status: hypovolaemic → IV isotonic saline; euvolaemic (SIADH) → fluid restriction; hypervolaemic → fluid + sodium restriction + treat heart failure/cirrhosis.
Hypertonic 3% NaCl is only for SEVERE SYMPTOMATIC acute hyponatremia — seizures, coma, respiratory compromise.
Monitor serum sodium every 2–4 hours during active correction; reduce frequency once target rate confirmed.
If Na rises faster than intended: administer D5W or DDAVP to halt correction and prevent ODS.

byContext.approach: Identify and treat precipitating cause. If hypovolaemic: oral rehydration solution (ORS) or IV 0.9% NaCl. Refer moderate-severe or symptomatic cases. Stop thiazide/causative drug. Check glucose to exclude hyperglycaemia.

drugs.name: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS)
drugs.notes: For hypovolaemic hyponatremia with mild symptoms. WHO-ORS contains 90 mEq/L Na — appropriate.

drugs.name: 0.9% Normal Saline
drugs.notes: Only for hypovolaemic hyponatremia. AVOID in euvolaemic or hypervolaemic — will worsen.

byContext.approach: Confirm diagnosis with urine Na/osmolality and serum osmolality. Fluid restrict if SIADH (500–750 mL/day). IV 0.9% NaCl if hypovolaemic. Monitor Na every 4–6 hours. Treat hypothyroidism, stop causative drugs.

drugs.name: 0.9% Normal Saline (isotonic)
drugs.notes: Monitor serum Na every 4–6h. Stop when euvolaemic. Target: +6–8 mEq/L in 24h.

drugs.name: Levothyroxine 50–100 mcg
drugs.dose: 50 mcg initially in elderly; 100 mcg in young adults
drugs.notes: Na corrects over 1–2 weeks as thyroid function normalises.

drugs.name: Fluid restriction
drugs.notes: Primary treatment for SIADH. Include all oral intake. Patient education essential.

byContext.approach: Above + ability to give 3% hypertonic saline for severe symptomatic cases under close monitoring. ICU admission for Na <120 with symptoms. Investigate and treat SIADH cause. Hydrocortisone if Addison's. DDAVP to slow over-rapid correction.

drugs.name: 3% Hypertonic Saline (NaCl)
drugs.route: IV (central line preferred; can use large peripheral IV)
drugs.frequency: May repeat 1–2 times for ongoing severe symptoms
drugs.duration: Until symptoms resolve; then switch to slow correction
drugs.notes: ONLY for seizures/coma/respiratory failure. Target +3–5 mEq/L to control acute symptoms. Monitor Na every 1–2h. NEVER use in chronic asymptomatic hyponatremia.

drugs.name: Hydrocortisone 100 mg
drugs.notes: If Addison's disease confirmed or strongly suspected. Do NOT delay for investigation in adrenal crisis.

drugs.name: DDAVP (Desmopressin) 2 mcg
drugs.duration: As needed to control correction rate
drugs.notes: Used with D5W to prevent over-rapid correction. Clamp strategy: give DDAVP to fix urine output, then give measured water to control Na rise precisely.

firstLine.intervention: Treat underlying cause (stop diuretic, treat TB, start levothyroxine, treat heart failure)

firstLine.note: First-line for SIADH. Sustained restriction over weeks is required.
Only for hypovolaemic hyponatremia. Monitor closely — beware overshoot.

secondLine.drug: Tolvaptan 15 mg oral once daily (vasopressin V2 receptor antagonist)
secondLine.note: For SIADH or hypervolaemic hyponatremia not responding to restriction. Expensive. Hepatotoxic with cirrhosis. Specialist use only.
Causes nephrogenic DI → free water excretion. Alternative when fluid restriction fails in SIADH. Not available widely in India.

secondLine.drug: Salt tablets 1–2 g three times daily
secondLine.note: Adjunct in SIADH with urine osmolality >500 — increases solute load, promotes water excretion.
Increases urine solute → forces water excretion. Effective for SIADH. Widely available, inexpensive — underutilised in India.

specialPopulations.modification: Higher risk of ODS from rapid correction. Limit correction to ≤8 mEq/L/24h (lower than standard). Risk factors: malnutrition, alcoholism, hypokalemia (common in elderly on diuretics). Monitor closely.
specialPopulations.caution: Many elderly patients have undiagnosed hypothyroidism — always check TSH.

specialPopulations.modification: Normal Na in pregnancy is slightly lower (132–136 mEq/L). Severe hyponatremia (<125) is dangerous for foetus. Treat hypovolaemia with isotonic saline. SIADH treatment: careful fluid restriction; avoid tolvaptan.
specialPopulations.caution: Oxytocin infusion (used in labour) has antidiuretic properties — can cause acute iatrogenic hyponatremia.

specialPopulations.modification: Children at higher risk of cerebral oedema from acute hyponatremia (tighter skull). Correction rate: same ≤10 mEq/L/24h but initial 3% bolus may be appropriate for seizures. Menstruating adolescent females at particularly high risk of cerebral herniation.
specialPopulations.caution: Post-operative children given hypotonic IV fluids are at very high risk — always use isotonic saline for maintenance.

specialPopulations.modification: Nephrotic syndrome and CKD: fluid and sodium restriction. Dialysis may be required in severe hyponatremia with renal failure.
specialPopulations.caution: Tolvaptan is contraindicated in anuria. Isotonic saline may not be excreted → volume overload.

specialPopulations.modification: Cirrhosis: primary treatment is underlying liver disease + diuretics (spironolactone). Fluid restrict to 1–1.5 L/day. Tolvaptan: SHORT TERM only — risk of hepatotoxicity in cirrhosis.
specialPopulations.caution: Avoid normal saline in cirrhosis — will worsen ascites and oedema.

monitoring.frequency: Every 1–2h during hypertonic saline infusion; every 4–6h during isotonic saline; every 6–12h during fluid restriction
monitoring.action: If rising faster than target: stop saline, administer D5W 5–10 mL/kg over 1–2h, consider DDAVP 2 mcg IV to clamp correction
Correct hypokalaemia aggressively — hypokalaemia exacerbates sodium correction and increases ODS risk
Falling urine osmolality suggests DDAVP loss or SIADH resolving — correction may accelerate
Improvement expected within hours. Any new neurological signs after starting correction → suspect ODS → stop, discuss urgently with nephrology
Strict I&O. Avoid free water in all cases except deliberate slowing of correction.

whenToRefer: Na <120 mEq/L or any severe symptoms (seizures, coma) → ICU/nephrology immediately
Suspected Addison's disease → endocrinology (urgent if adrenal crisis)
SIADH with unclear aetiology → investigate for malignancy (especially small cell lung cancer)
Failure to correct with standard management or paradoxical worsening
Suspected osmotic demyelination syndrome (new neurological signs during correction) → neurology + nephrology
Cirrhosis with recurrent hyponatremia → hepatology (consider tolvaptan, liver transplant evaluation)
Chronic SIADH requiring long-term tolvaptan or urea therapy → nephrology/endocrinology
pitfalls: Correcting sodium faster than 10–12 mEq/L in 24 hours in chronic hyponatremia → risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS/CPM) — irreversible
Giving hypotonic saline (0.45% NaCl or D5W) to a hypovolaemic patient → worsens hyponatremia
Giving isotonic saline to a SIADH patient → urine Na concentration exceeds infused Na concentration → paradoxical fall in serum sodium
Missing adrenal insufficiency (Addison's) as a cause → patient develops adrenal crisis if only sodium is treated
Failing to stop diuretics before sodium correction — ongoing urinary Na loss will confound and accelerate correction unpredictably
Not correcting hypokalemia — potassium correction raises serum sodium (K+ replaces intracellular Na+) and must be counted toward the 10 mEq/L daily correction limit
Using 3% saline in chronic asymptomatic hyponatremia — will cause ODS without benefit
Not monitoring for overshoot — many patients self-correct after dDAVP is stopped or diuretic withdrawn; check Na every 4–6h in the first 24h

pearls: In India, always ask about anti-TB medications — SIADH from TB (meningitis or pulmonary) and SIADH from rifampicin are both common and correctable.
The sodium content of potassium chloride supplementation adds to the daily sodium correction — account for K+ when calculating the safe daily limit.
A patient who self-corrects (e.g., thiazide stopped, SIADH resolving) can overshoot the safe rate without any active intervention — always recheck Na 4–6h after stopping the precipitant.
Urea 30 g/day dissolved in water or juice is an effective, cheap, and well-tolerated treatment for SIADH in India when fluid restriction fails — largely underused.

Beer potomania is a clinical trap: low Na despite large urine volumes — the urine is maximally dilute but the low-solute diet prevents adequate free water excretion. Treatment: increase dietary protein and sodium, not fluid restriction alone.
Post-operative hyponatremia is almost always SIADH from pain, nausea, and hypotonic IV fluids — switch to 0.9% NaCl maintenance immediately post-op in all surgical patients.
pitfalls: The single most dangerous mistake: correcting chronic hyponatremia faster than 10 mEq/L/24h. This causes osmotic demyelination syndrome — quadriplegia, dysarthria, or death — and is irreversible.

Giving normal saline to a SIADH patient thinking 'isotonic saline is safe' — it is NOT. The kidney concentrates the infusate and wastes the sodium, causing net water retention and worsening hyponatremia.
Not checking TSH in every patient with euvolaemic hyponatremia — hypothyroidism is common in India, easily treatable, and exactly mimics SIADH.
Ignoring hypokalaemia during correction — every 1 mEq/L rise in K+ raises Na by approximately 1 mEq/L. Potassium replacement counts toward daily sodium correction limit.
Treating the sodium without treating the cause — the patient goes home and returns in 2 weeks with the same problem.

keyTakeaway: Hyponatremia is a water excess problem, not a sodium deficit problem — management requires understanding why ADH is elevated or water intake is excessive.
Volume status assessment (hypovolaemic vs euvolaemic vs hypervolaemic) is the mandatory first step and determines whether to give saline, restrict fluid, or treat an underlying organ failure.
The rate of correction is paramount: never exceed 10–12 mEq/L per 24 hours in chronic hyponatremia — osmotic demyelination syndrome is an irreversible iatrogenic catastrophe.
In India, TB-related SIADH, thiazide diuretics, hypothyroidism, and diarrhoeal dehydration are the most common causes — a systematic approach to aetiology prevents recurrence.
3% hypertonic saline is a life-saving emergency tool for symptomatic severe acute hyponatremia — but its use in chronic asymptomatic hyponatremia is one of the most dangerous errors in medicine.
